coredata.dc:title: Boric Acid-Catalyzed Synthesis: A scalable strategy for High-Yield and Tunable red fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Nanzhi
Yang, Yuchen
Huang, Wenhao
Zhang, Jianting
Chen, Guohua
coredata.dc:description: Red fluorescent carbon dots (RCDs) have garnered significant attention in the fields of lighting and bioimaging due to their exceptional optical properties and favorable biocompatibility. However, existing synthesis strategies often involve complex processes and rely on expensive, toxic polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, which fundamentally limit their yield and applicability. In this study, RCDs with a high quantum yield of 45 % were synthesized via a one-step solvothermal method using citric acid and urea as precursors, with boric acid as a catalyst. The introduction of boric acid induced a red shift in the fluorescence emission wavelength of the carbon dots from 468 nm to 651 nm. The yield of high-purity RCDs extracted from the crude product increased from 1 % to 55.4 %, and they could be easily separated via centrifugation. These RCDs significantly enhanced the color rendering performance of white light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and demonstrated satisfactory biocompatibility for cell imaging applications. Notably, the catalytic activity of boric acid was also observed in the synthesis of RCDs from other precursors, resulting in varying degrees of red shift in the fluorescence emission wavelength. This study establishes a foundation for environmentally sustainable, cost-effective, and scalable synthesis of RCDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Boric acid catalysis
High yield
LED
Biological imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University (Grant no. ZQN-918). Graphene Powder & Composite Materials Research Center of Fujian Province (2017H2001). The Xiamen Key Laboratory of Polymers & Electronic Materials, grant number 2024-01. Nanzhi Zheng & Yuchen Yang conceived and designed the research, investigation, data analysis, and editing. Wenhao Huang performed TEM and AFM characterizations. Jianting Zhang supervised the work and analyzed the data. Guohua Chen performed funding acquisition, review, editing, and project administration. The manuscript was written through the contributions of all authors. These authors jointly supervised this work: Jianting Zhang & Guohua Chen. These authors contributed equally: Nanzhi Zheng & Yuchen Yang

This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University (Grant no. ZQN-918). Graphene Powder & Composite Materials Research Center of Fujian Province ( 2017H2001 ). The Xiamen Key Laboratory of Polymers & Electronic Materials, grant number 2024-01 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of C-CDs (a) and B-CDs (b) and their particle size distribution. High-resolution TEM images for C-CDs (c) and B-CDs (d) and its corresponding FFT and lattice fringe images. AFM image of C-CDs (e) and B-CDs (f).

(a, b) Photographs of B-CDs (a) and C-CDs (b) under ambient sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of C-CDs and B-CDs. (d) Fluorescence spectra of C-CDs and B-CDs. (e) Emission spectra of B-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (f) Time-resolved PL spectrum of B-CDs and C-CDs. (g, h) Absorption edge (inset) and UPS spectra of B-CDs (g) and C-CDs (h).
XPS survey spectra of C-CDs (a) and B-CDs (b). High-resolution C 1 s spectra of C-CDs (a) and B-CDs (b). High-resolution O 1 s spectra of C-CDs (a) and B-CDs (b).
(a) Raman spectra of C-CDs and B-CDs. (b) XRD patterns of C-CDs and B-CDs. (c) FT-IR spectrum of C-CDs and B-CDs.
The schematic representation of the formation of RCDs in the boric acid-catalyzed system.
(a) Photographs of 10 samples 365 nm UV light. (b-f) Fluorescence spectra of 10 samples.

(a) EL spectrum and optical images(inset) of the WLED lamp. (b) Optical micrographs of L929 cells treated with B-CDs-PVP for 24 h. (c) Fluorescence imaging of L929 cells treated with B-CDs-PVP (100 μg·mL-1) for 24 h, λex = 488 nm.

head.ce:title: Boric Acid-Catalyzed Synthesis: A scalable strategy for High-Yield and Tunable red fluorescent carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red fluorescent carbon dots (RCDs) have garnered significant attention in the fields of lighting and bioimaging due to their exceptional optical properties and favorable biocompatibility. However, existing synthesis strategies often involve complex processes and rely on expensive, toxic polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, which fundamentally limit their yield and applicability. In this study, RCDs with a high quantum yield of 45 % were synthesized via a one-step solvothermal method using citric acid and urea as precursors, with boric acid as a catalyst. The introduction of boric acid induced a red shift in the fluorescence emission wavelength of the carbon dots from 468 nm to 651 nm. The yield of high-purity RCDs extracted from the crude product increased from 1 % to 55.4 %, and they could be easily separated via centrifugation. These RCDs significantly enhanced the color rendering performance of white light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and demonstrated satisfactory biocompatibility for cell imaging applications. Notably, the catalytic activity of boric acid was also observed in the synthesis of RCDs from other precursors, resulting in varying degrees of red shift in the fluorescence emission wavelength. This study establishes a foundation for environmentally sustainable, cost-effective, and scalable synthesis of RCDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor quantum dots represent a cutting-edge class of nanomaterials that have garnered considerable attention due to their high fluorescence quantum yield. Unlike other quantum dots, which often have significant environmental impacts and rely on non-renewable resources, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a promising alternative in the field of semiconductor quantum dots [1]. Carbon dots have garnered increasing interest due to their wide range of raw material sources, superior biocompatibility, and versatile surface functionalization capabilities. These properties make carbon dots highly suitable for a wide range of applications, including biological imaging, optoelectronic devices, energy storage, sensing, and drug delivery [2–4].

Among various types of CDs, red fluorescent carbon dots (RCDs) with low cytotoxicity are particularly advantageous for fluorescence imaging [5–7]. Unlike CDs with shorter excitation wavelengths, whose efficacy is significantly compromised by the autofluorescence of biomolecules, RCDs exhibit superior performance in cellular and tissue imaging due to their enhanced tissue penetration, minimal phototoxicity, and reduced background autofluorescence in biological samples [8,9]. Furthermore, recent advancements in warm white light-emitting diode (WLED) systems, distinguished by a high color rendering index (CRI > 80), have garnered considerable scholarly interest. Red fluorescent materials play a pivotal role in enhancing the red spectral component of WLEDs, thereby lowering the correlated color temperature (CCT) of WLEDs to create a more visually comfortable lighting environment and alleviate eye strain [10–12].

CDs have been synthesized through various methods utilizing a broad range of precursor materials [13]. However, most reported CDs exhibit strong blue or green luminescence, with relatively few emitting fluorescence at wavelengths above 630 nm. Additionally, these long-wavelength fluorescent CDs generally possess low quantum yields and limited production efficiencies [14–16]. Recent investigations have revealed that the fluorescence of RCDs is primarily attributed to larger particle sizes, particularly the expansion of internal sp2 carbon domains [17]. To this end, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) have been adopted as precursors for RCDs synthesis. With their extensive π-conjugated structures, PAHs promote the formation of highly conjugated sp2 domains, which are essential for red fluorescence [18,19]. Nevertheless, the practical application of PAHs is restricted due to their scarcity, high cost, and toxicity. Examples include diaminonaphthalene, dihydroxynaphthalene [20,21], 1,1′-binaphthyl-2,2′-diamine [22], perylene [23], and perylene-3,9-dicarboxylic acid [24]. However, the practical application of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) is significantly constrained by their scarcity, high cost, and toxicity, presenting considerable challenges for large-scale and sustainable synthesis. There is limited literature on the synthesis of RCDs using non-aromatic compounds as precursors, primarily due to the complex reactions that occur during the carbon dot synthesis process. These reactions lead to structural complexity and heterogeneity in surface states, resulting in a mixture of carbon dots with varying sizes and optical properties. Typically, column chromatography is required to isolate RCDs, a method that is not only time-consuming but also yields extremely low quantities of RCDs [25]. Some researchers have attempted to modulate the optical properties of carbon dots by introducing catalysts. For instance, Niu et al. utilized anhydrous sodium bisulfate as a catalyst to facilitate the dehydration and condensation of reaction precursors, thereby expanding the internal sp2 carbon core of the carbon dots and shifting the fluorescence emission peak from 460 nm to 600 nm [26]. Similarly, Jiao et al. employed polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and ascorbic acid (AA) as green reagents, with metal ions (Fe3+ or Cu2+) as catalysts, which not only enhanced the fluorescence intensity but also extended the fluorescence lifetime [27]. However, reports on synthesizing RCDs using catalysts are still limited, highlighting the pressing need to develop more efficient and high-yield methods for RCD production. Ideally, these methods would avoid the use of costly and toxic precursors and eliminate lengthy purification steps, making the production process more practical and scalable for a wider range of applications.

In this study, boric acid was employed as a catalyst alongside citric acid and urea as precursors to efficiently synthesize RCDs via a solvothermal reaction. The catalytic efficiency of boric acid significantly enhanced the RCDs content in the crude product, increasing from less than 1 % to 55.4 %. The synthesized red fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) possess an extensive conjugated structure, enabling the acquisition of high-purity red fluorescent carbon dots through simple centrifugation. These B-CDs exhibit a fluorescence emission peak centered at 651 nm and demonstrate a quantum yield of up to 45 %, indicating substantial potential for applications in reducing the correlated color temperature (CCT) of white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) and in biofluorescence imaging. This boric acid catalytic strategy enables the efficient synthesis of high-quality RCDs without relying on fused-ring structures as precursors. Furthermore, this boric acid-catalyzed synthesis method for RCDs can be applied to other preparation techniques, effectively enhancing the fluorescence emission wavelength of carbon dots when using different precursors. This work provides a universal strategy for RCD production and is expected to offer critical technical support for the future high-efficiency synthesis of RCDs, thereby paving the way for advancements in CDs technology.

A series of orthogonal experiments with single-variable control were designed using citric acid, urea, and boric acid as precursors. Key synthesis parameters, including boric acid dosage, reaction time, and temperature, were systematically varied to investigate their effects. The crude products obtained via solvothermal reaction (Table S1) were initially screened by evaluating their red fluorescence emission under UV illumination (Fig. S1), providing a preliminary indication of RCDs yield. Subsequent fluorescence spectroscopy analysis of the dialyzed CD mixtures revealed two distinct emission peaks at ∼ 450 nm and ∼ 630 nm (Fig. S2). Simultaneously, the dialyzed carbon dot mixture was centrifuged to collect the RCDs, and the centrifugation yield is shown in Fig. S3. Taking into account both the fluorescence peak intensity and the product yield, the highest efficiency was obtained under solvothermal conditions at 180 °C for 12 h with the addition of 2 g of boric acid. Finally, the B-CDs sample was prepared according to Scheme 1 . For comparison, a parallel synthesis without boric acid was conducted to produce control carbon dots (C-CDs). The crude product from the solvothermal reaction without boric acid exhibited markedly different fluorescence under identical conditions (Fig. S4), highlighting the pivotal role of boric acid in the formation of B-CDs. To further elucidate the influence of boron incorporation, the same purification procedure was applied to the comparative samples to obtain C-CDs.

As illustrated in Fig. 1 a and b, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images demonstrate that both C-CDs and B-CDs have good dispersion with uniformly distributed particle sizes. The particle size distribution of carbon dots was statistically analyzed by randomly selecting 50 positions, and the corresponding statistical analysis is illustrated in Fig. 1e and 1f. The particle size distribution for C-CDs ranges from 1.2 nm to 3.6 nm, yielding an average diameter of 2.1 nm. In contrast, the B-CDs display particle sizes ranging from 2.5 nm to 4.1 nm, with an average diameter of 3.1 nm. It is evident from the figures that the particle size of B-CDs is significantly larger than that of C-CDs.

High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (insets in Fig. 1c and 1d) reveal that both C-CDs and B-CDs exhibit a lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphene[28]. This indicates that C-CDs and B-CDs share the same internal core structure, which is similar to that of graphite. Notably, the B-CDs display clearer and more continuous lattice fringes, indicative of higher crystallinity compared to the C-CDs, where the lattice fringes appear more defective. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) images of the selected areas (insets in Fig. 1c and 1d) further corroborate the enhanced crystallinity of the graphene structures in the B-CDs [29]. Furthermore, atomic force microscopy (AFM) images (Fig. 2 e and 2f) reveal that the heights of C-CDs and B-CDs are 0.7 nm and 1.1 nm, respectively. This indicates that C-CDs are composed of approximately two graphene layers, whereas B-CDs consist of about three graphene layers. A noticeable increase in the size of B-CDs and an enhancement in their internal graphene-like core structure can be clearly observed. Therefore, it can be inferred that the addition of boric acid intensifies the carbonization process between precursor molecules, leading to a reduction in internal defects, higher crystallinity, and an increase in the conjugated structure of the carbon core. These changes ultimately lead to a narrowing of the band gap. Furthermore, the increase in the size of carbon dots under the quantum size effect also contributes to a red shift in the fluorescence wavelength. These factors collectively account for the observed changes in the fluorescence emission of the carbon dots.

B-CDs and C-CDs exhibit different optical properties. As shown in Fig. 2a and b, the B-CDs under the UV light illumination showed bright red fluorescence, while the C-CDs sample showed white light instead of red emission. The absorption spectra and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of B-CDs are red-shifted to longer wavelengths compared to C-CDs (Fig. 2c, 2d). Specifically, the emission peak of B-CDs is centered at 651 nm in DMF solution, with a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 45.22 % under 595 nm excitation (Fig. S4), whereas C-CDs do not exhibit red emission. The photoluminescence of B-CDs could be excited over a broad range of wavelengths, and the maximum emission peak position is virtually independent of the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2e). This behavior suggests that the fluorescence of B-CDs originates from uniform fluorescence centers, primarily through band-edge excitons. Additionally, the peak wavelength of the PL excitation spectrum aligns well with the corresponding excitonic absorption peak wavelength (Fig. 2a), indicating that the emission originates from band-edge exciton-state decay [30]. As shown in the absorption spectra in Fig. 2c, the λedge values of C-CDs and B-CDs are determined to be 500 nm and 680 nm, respectively. Based on the Kubelka-Munk theory, plots of (αhν)1/2 versus hν are displayed in the insets of Fig. 2g and 2 h. Using the tangent method, the band gaps of C-CDs and B-CDs were calculated to be 2.24 eV and 1.92 eV, respectively, indicating a reduction in band gap upon the introduction of boric acid as a catalyst. Furthermore, ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurements were conducted on the C-CDs and B-CDs (Fig. 2g and 2 h). The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy levels of C-CDs and B-CDs were calculated to be − 3.51 eV and − 4.35 eV, respectively, using the formula EVB = − [21.22− (Efermi – Eend)]. By combining these HOMO energy levels with the optical band gaps obtained from the absorption spectra, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels were determined to be − 1.27 eV and − 2.43 eV for C-CDs and B-CDs, respectively [31,32]. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) analysis revealed that the fluorescence lifetimes of C-CDs and B-CDs were 2.21 ns and 4.45 ns, respectively, which are characteristic of carbon dot emissions and significantly longer than the sub-nanosecond lifetimes typically associated with free organic molecules. The extended luminescence lifetime of B-CDs can be attributed to variations in core states, which modulate defect-state emission, with luminescence lifetimes increasing as graphitization intensifies [33]. Compared with previous reports on red-emissive carbon dots synthesized from citric acid (Table S2), this study demonstrates clear advantages in terms of quantum yield, fluorescence wavelength, and other related properties.

The survey X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the C-CDs and B-CDs samples are presented in Fig. 3 a and b. The three characteristic peaks at binding energies of 285 eV, 399 eV, and 532 eV correspond to the C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s orbitals, respectively. Notably, the absence of a peak around 187 eV in the XPS spectrum of the B-CDs sample indicates that boron was not incorporated into the carbon dot structure, thereby confirming the role of boric acid as a catalyst rather than a precursor in the formation of B-CDs. The relative atomic ratio of carbon increases from 67.54 % in the C-CDs sample to 75.58 % in the B-CDs sample, values significantly higher than those in the precursor molecules, where citric acid contains 46 % carbon and urea contains 25 % carbon. This increase in carbon content suggests an enhanced degree of dehydration and carbonization of the precursors during the solvothermal synthesis when boric acid is used as a catalyst.

The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. 3c and 3d) exhibit peaks at binding energies of 284.6 eV, 285.4 eV, and 288.6 eV, corresponding to C–C/C=C, C–O/C–N, and C=O/C=N bonds, respectively.[34] Compared to the C-CDs, the B-CDs show reduced relative intensities in the peaks associated with C–N/C–O–C and C=O/C=N bonds, further indicating an increased degree of dehydration and carbonization in the B-CDs synthesized with boric acid as the catalyst.

The high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectra of the C-CDs and B-CDs (Fig. 3e and 3f) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 530.9 eV and 532.4 eV, corresponding to C=O and C–OH/C–O–C functional groups, respectively. The increased intensity of the C–OH/C–O–C peak and the decreased intensity of the C=O peak in B-CDs relative to C-CDs suggest a transformation of carbonyl groups into other oxygen-containing groups, supporting the occurrence of boric acid-induced enolization. That further validates the role of boric acid as an effective catalyst in promoting the dehydration and carbonization processes, facilitating the formation of CDs.

For quantitative X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis, both C-CDs and B-CDs were mixed with 10 wt% nano silicon powder. As shown in Fig. 4 a, a reflection peak centered around 26.8° is observed, corresponding to the (002) plane of the graphite lattice [34–36]. Compared to the peak strength of the silicon powder, the graphite peak intensity of the B-CDs is stronger than that of the C-CDs. This observation suggests a higher degree of graphitization in the B-CDs.

In the Raman spectrum (Fig. 4b), both B-CDs and C-CDs show characteristic disorder-induced D bands around 1360 cm−1 and graphitic G bands around 1600 cm−1. The intensity ratios (ID/IG) are approximately 0.95 and 1.12 for the B-CDs and C-CDs, respectively, suggesting that B-CDs have a higher degree of crystallinity [37]. This observation further supports the enhanced graphitization achieved with boric acid catalysis.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was employed further to characterize the functional groups present in C-CDs and B-CDs. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 4c) of both B-CDs and C-CDs show absorption bands at 3450 cm−1, attributed to hydroxyl groups, and at 1655 cm−1, corresponding to aromatic C=C stretching [38]. Notably, C-CDs display more pronounced stretching vibrations of C=O at 1726 cm−1, indicating a greater presence of carbonyl groups compared to B-CDs. Additionally, the aliphatic C–H stretching vibrations (2960–2853 cm−1) and aliphatic C–N stretching vibrations at 1280 cm−1 in the B-CDs are significantly reduced [39,40]. These changes in peak intensities confirmed modifications in the functional groups of the proposed structures. The decrease in the intensity of oxygen-containing functional group peaks suggested the enolization of carbonyl compounds, leading to a reduction of these groups and an expansion of π-conjugation structures in B-CDs.

In summary, the large-sized conjugated sp2 clusters of B-CDs were responsible for the pronounced red emission. The utilization of boric acid as a catalyst played a critical role in forming the unique structure of B-CDs, which exhibited a high degree of carbonization and a red shift in the fluorescence wavelength. According to the reported works, citric acid and urea would assemble into a nanoplate structure through intermolecular hydrogen bonding and subsequently form sp2-conjugated structures via dehydration and carbonization [30,41]. Following dehydration, sp2-conjugated structures enriched with carbonyl groups encounter challenges in further dehydration and carbonization due to insufficient hydroxyl groups. These smaller sp2-conjugated structures undergo π-π stacking and subsequently assemble into carbon dots. However, under basic conditions that favor the formation of tetrahedral neutral or anionic borates, boric acid facilitates the enolization of carbonyl compounds [42,43]. This process enables smaller sp2-conjugated structures containing hydroxyl and carbonyl groups to undergo further dehydration, resulting in the growth of larger conjugated structures. The expansion of these conjugated structures decreases the bandgap of the carbon dots, inducing a red shift in their photoluminescence. (Fig. 5 ). To investigate the role of boric acid in the reaction, we performed DFT calculations based on the proposed carbon dot structural model. The results indicate that boric acid catalyzes the enolization process by significantly lowering the activation energy of the intermediate transformation. Compared with the reaction without boric acid, the formation of a tetrahedral borate anion-through the interaction of boric acid with the sp2-conjugated structure of the carbon dots-greatly reduces the Gibbs free energy of the system (Fig. S5). This facilitates further reaction between intermediates and reactants, thereby promoting the continued growth of the sp2-conjugated domains. To verify the proposed enolization mechanism induced by boric acid, we conducted solvothermal reactions for 4 h and 8 h to obtain partially reacted intermediates, denoted as B-CDs-4H and B-CDs-8H, respectively. These intermediates were characterized by FTIR spectroscopy. Compared with fully reacted B-CDs and the control sample C-CDs, the intermediates exhibited similar structural features to B-CDs. Notably, the characteristic peaks corresponding to carbonyl (C=O) and C–H groups were significantly reduced. Additionally, we observed a gradual redshift of the C=C stretching vibration band-associated with the sp2 carbon framework-from 1661 cm−1 to 1636 cm−1 (Fig. S6). This transformation indicates a reduction in carbonyl content and an increase in C=C bonds within the carbon dot structure, further supporting the role of boric acid in promoting enolization.

To ascertain whether this mechanism could be extrapolated to the synthesis of carbon dots using alternative precursors, we conducted analogous experiments with a variety of other starting materials, including 2-aminomalonamide, thiocarbamide, oxamide, phloroglucinol, and p-phenylenediamine (Table S3). Five sets of comparative samples were obtained based on the presence or absence of boric acid, with their images under 365 nm ultraviolet light depicted in Fig. 6 a. The fluorescence of all samples altered upon the addition of boric acid. According to the fluorescence spectra illustrated in Fig. 6b-6f, the fluorescence emission peaks of the five samples with added boric acid exhibited varying degrees of red shift. The results demonstrate that the strategy of employing boric acid as a catalyst to enhance the fluorescence wavelength of carbon dots can be extended to other synthetic methodologies.

The wide spectral region of C-CDs enables them to emit white fluorescence, while the strong red fluorescence and excellent photostability of B-CDs render them particularly advantageous for generating warm white light with a high CRI. Two types of carbon dot phosphors were prepared by encapsulating and dispersing C-CDs and B-CDs within melamine–formaldehyde (MF) resin. Due to the good dispersion of C-CDs and B-CDs in the MF powder, these carbon dot phosphors emit brilliant light when excited under UV illumination. Leveraging the efficient properties of C-CDs-MF and B-CDs-MF phosphors, a series of luminescent light-emitting diodes (LEDs) were fabricated by varying the proportions of these phosphors. Specifically, four types of LEDs were prepared: LED1 (10:0), LED2 (8:2), LED3 (6:4), and LED4 (0:10), where the ratios represent the proportion of C-CDs-MF to B-CDs-MF encapsulated onto UV LED chips (365 nm) using epoxy resin. As seen from the LED emission spectrum in Fig. 7 a, the intensity of the red fluorescent band increases significantly with the increasing B-CDs content, shifting from a cold WLED (LED1) to a warm WLED (LED2). Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. S7, the incorporation of B-CDs-MF phosphors enables the tuning of the correlated color temperature (CCT) of the WLED from a cold temperature of 6036 K to a warm temperature of 4536 K under a working current of 0.1 A and a voltage of 3 V. Concurrently, the CIE color coordinates shift from (0.32, 0.38) to (0.3682, 0.4126). Additionally, the prepared red LEDs (LED4) had similar fluorescence emission peaks to the B-CDs solution, and their CIE coordinates were located at (0.44, 0.28). The optical images of LEDs operating at a current of 0.1 A are depicted in the inset of Fig. 7a. Two photographs of fruits taken under the illumination of these two types of lamps are provided in Fig. S8. Clearly, the warm WLED lamp is more capable of showing the true colors of the fruits. Additionally, the CRI of the warm WLED lamp achieves a remarkable value of 91.5, underscoring the efficacy of the B-CDs in enhancing the quality of emitted light. The luminous efficacy of WLED was evaluated under varying driving currents (20, 30, 40, 50, 100, 150, and 200 mA). A peak efficacy of 11.24 lm/W was achieved at 100 mA. Furthermore, long-term stability tests demonstrated excellent operational durability, with the device maintaining stable emission after 80 h of continuous operation at 100 mA while retaining 84 % of its initial luminous efficacy (Fig. S9).

The synthesized B-CDs were also applied for cell imaging. In order to mitigate the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching, these hydrophobic B-CDs were coated with Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), referred to as B-CDs-PVPs phosphors (Fig. S10). The cytotoxicity of B-CDs-PVPs was assessed using the CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. S11, the B-CDs-PVPs exhibited negligible cytotoxicity against the L929 cells cultured for 24 h at concentrations ranging from 0 to 200 μg·mL−1. Even at concentrations exceeding 100 μg·mL−1, the cell survival rate remained above 90 % following 24 h of treatment with B-CDs-PVPs, indicating a favorable biocompatibility. When the incubation period was extended to 48 h (Fig. S12), cellular viability remained stable across all concentrations, suggesting promising potential for long-term biological applications. Subsequently, the fluorescence microscopy image of B-CDs-PVPs treated L929 cells was achieved under an excitation wavelength of 488 nm (Fig. 7b,7c).

In conclusion, we have successfully designed a novel method for synthesizing highly efficient RCDs using boric acid as a catalyst. The resulting B-CDs demonstrate high purity, a fluorescence emission wavelength of 651 nm, and a quantum yield (QY) of up to 45 %. Both experimental and theoretical studies indicate that boric acid facilitates the enolization of carbonyl compounds, thereby enhancing the dehydration and carbonization of small organic molecules. This catalytic process promotes the formation and growth of carbon dots by expanding the sp2 carbon core, resulting in a higher yield of carbon dots by significant π-conjugation. Additionally, the implementation of boric acid catalysis significantly increased the proportion of red-emissive carbon dots in the crude product from less than 1 % to 55.4 %, effectively overcoming the issue of low yields associated with RCDs. Furthermore, we explored various other molecules like phloroglucinol, p-aniline, and thiocarbamide as precursors for CDs synthesis, verifying the validity of boric acid catalytic strategy for the green, economical, facile synthesis of RCDs with high efficiency and yield. The boric acid catalytic method can significantly enhance the yield of RCDs. However, the scalability of this method remains constrained by the inherent limitations of solvothermal synthesis. We anticipate that transferring this improved boric acid catalytic strategy to other higher-yield synthesis methods will enable a major breakthrough in the large-scale production of RCDs.

Materials: All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Citric acid, urea, paraformaldehyde, oxamide, polyvinylpyrrolidone, 2-aminomalonamide, phloroglucinol, thiocarbamide and Rhodamine 101 were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Boric acid, ethylenediamine, melamine, p-aniline, and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China).

Synthesis of C-CDs and B-CDs. Citric acid (2 g), urea (0.6 g), and boric acid (2 g) were dissolved in 25 mL of dimethylformamide (DMF) and subjected to solvothermal treatment at 180 °C for 12 h. Upon completion, the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature. The resulting solution was dialyzed against deionized water using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 1,000 Da to remove small molecular impurities. The contents of the dialysis bags were subsequently collected, centrifuged, and washed with deionized water three times. Finally, the product was freeze-dried for 48 h to yield B-CDs in a black powder form. For comparison, control carbon dots (C-CDs) were synthesized using the same procedure, omitting the addition of boric acid.

Preparation of C-CDs-MF and B-CDs-MF phosphors: B-CDs (5 mg), melamine (2.5 g), and paraformaldehyde (3 g) were mixed in 100 mL of water and heated to 50 °C under magnetic stirring for 40 min. The resulting solution was then freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain the B-CDs-MF phosphors. For comparison, C-CDs-MF phosphors were prepared using the same method by substituting B-CDs with C-CDs.

Preparation of B-CDs-PVP phosphors: Finely dispersed B-CDs-PVP phosphors were prepared by mixing B-CDs (2 mg) with an aqueous polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) solution (10 mL) at a concentration of 100 mg·mL-1. The mixture was magnetically stirred for 1 h to ensure uniform dispersion and then freeze-dried to obtain the desired phosphor powders.

Fabrication of WLEDs: To prepare the white light-emitting diode (WLED), a mixed powder of C-CDs-MF and B-CDs-MF phosphors (0.1 g) in varying proportions was dispersed into epoxy resin (0.8 g). Subsequently, ethylenediamine (0.1 ml) was added to the mixture, which was then coated onto a UV LED chip. The assembly was allowed to cure at room temperature for 24 h, resulting in the formation of the WLED device.

Cell compatibility of the B-CDs: The biocompatibility of the B-CDs was analyzed using a Cell Counting Kit (CCK)-8 (Keygen) assay. Briefly, L929 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well overnight for proper cell attachment. Then, the B-CDs suspension was added to the corresponding wells with different concentrations. Cell viability was calculated as below: Cell viability (%) = (sample group OD 450 mm- blank group OD450 mm)/(positive control group OD450 nm − blank group OD450 mm)x 100. Correspondingly, the images of L929 cells treated with 100 μg/ml B-CDs were captured by a fluorescence microscope under an excitation wavelength of 488 nm (Axio Observer Al, ZEISS, Oberkochen, Germany).

Characterization: The morphological analysis of the C-CDs and B-CDs nanostructures was conducted using a FEI Talos F200X G2 transmission electron microscope (TEM). Changes in the absorbance of the samples were measured with a Thermo Fisher Evolution 201 UV–vis spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer equipped with an Al K-alpha monochromatic X-ray source and a pass energy of 150 eV. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was conducted using a Nicolet iS50 spectrometer to examine the molecular structures and bonding characteristics of the samples. Raman spectra were obtained using a Renishaw inVia spectrophotometer with 532 nm excitation. Fluorescence spectra, quantum yield (QY), and lifetime measurements were recorded with a Thermo Fisher Lumina fluorescence spectrophotometer and an Edinburgh Instruments FLS1000-DD-stm fluorescence spectrophotometer, the latter equipped with a calibrated integrating sphere. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected using a Rigaku MiniFlex600 diffractometer for lattice structure analysis of the samples. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurements were conducted on a Thermo Fisher Scientific Nexsa spectrometer with a He I gas discharge lamp (hν = 21.22 eV). The samples for UPS were prepared by spin-coating on indium tin oxide (ITO) substrates. Cellular imaging was performed with a ZEISS Axio Observer A1 microscope.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University (Grant no. ZQN-918). Graphene Powder & Composite Materials Research Center of Fujian Province (2017H2001). The Xiamen Key Laboratory of Polymers & Electronic Materials, grant number 2024-01.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanzhi Zheng & Yuchen Yang conceived and designed the research, investigation, data analysis, and editing. Wenhao Huang performed TEM and AFM characterizations. Jianting Zhang supervised the work and analyzed the data. Guohua Chen performed funding acquisition, review, editing, and project administration. The manuscript was written through the contributions of all authors.
These authors jointly supervised this work: Jianting Zhang & Guohua Chen.
These authors contributed equally: Nanzhi Zheng & Yuchen Yang

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126562

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
